aquacropr 0.2.0

Onset of the Growing Season: Methods, Limitations, and Sahelian Realities

Source: vignettes/sowingdate.Rmd
sowingdate.Rmd

Background and design rationale

A limitation of the AquaCrop standalone

On the .CAL file. The .CAL file works as intended in the AquaCrop GUI, which appears to use it to derive sowing dates before writing the project master file (.PRM). In standalone mode, the exact mechanism is not documented — but the sowing date embedded in the .PRM is the only input AquaCrop needs to anchor the growing period. Whether or not the standalone re-evaluates the onset rule at runtime is therefore, in practice, irrelevant.

The aquacropr approach

What aquacropr does is straightforward: find_onset() reads the .CAL, computes the sowing date series from the climate data, and write_prm() writes those dates directly into the .PRM. The standalone receives exactly what it expects. By owning this computation in R, aquacropr is free to implement any onset rule that can be expressed as a function of daily climate data — the four native AquaCrop criteria are fully reproduced, and three additional methods designed for rainfall-limited environments are added on top.

You do not need to call find_onset() for simulations to work. When you pass a .CAL to write_prm() and launch a simulation with run_aquacrop(), onset dates are computed and embedded automatically. find_onset() is provided so you can inspect, validate, and compare onset dates before committing to a configuration — a transparent window into the planting-date engine, not a required step.

The typical workflow is:

library(aquacropr)

# 1. Choose and encode your onset rule
write_cal(
  site_name       = "Ouaga",
  onset           = "rainfall",
  window_start    = 100,
  window_length   = 120,
  criterion       = 2,
  preset_value    = 20,
  successive_days = 3
)

# 2. (Optional but recommended) Inspect the resulting onset dates
find_onset("Ouaga")
#>   year onset_doy onset_date
#> 1 1981       147 1981-05-27
#> 2 1982       153 1982-06-02
#> ...

# 3. Pass the .CAL to write_prm() — everything else is automatic
write_prm(site_name = "Ouaga", ...)

Novel implementations in aquacropr

The three Sahelian methods below are exclusively available in aquacropr. They are not accessible through the AquaCrop standalone GUI and are not part of the official AquaCrop criterion set.

Method Origin Available in AquaCrop standalone
Native criteria 1–4 (rainfall) AquaCrop / FAO ✅ Yes
Native criteria 1–4 (thermal) AquaCrop / FAO ✅ Yes
Sivakumar (1988) Agroclimatology literature ❌ aquacropr only
Marteau et al. (2009) Agroclimatology literature ❌ aquacropr only
Fuzzy logic — Criterion 5 (Waongo et al., 2014) Agroclimatology literature ❌ aquacropr only

Their inclusion is a direct consequence of the reimplementation strategy: once onset detection is in R, adding a new method is a matter of implementing a new decision function — no recompilation, no GUI patch required.

Why onset matters

In rainfed agriculture, the onset of the growing season is the most consequential decision a farmer makes each year. Sow too early and a dry spell after germination can be fatal. Sow too late and the season is too short to reach maturity. In the Sahel, where the rainy season lasts 90 to 150 days and rainfall is highly variable in time and space, the planting date alone can explain yield differences of 20% or more between identical fields in the same year (Waongo et al., 2014).

Getting the onset rule right is therefore not a technical detail — it is the agronomic foundation of your simulation.

The three onset types

write_cal() supports three onset values, each determining which climate file find_onset() will read at runtime.

onset Climate file Typical use case
"fixed" .PLU (years only) Fixed sowing calendar, irrigated systems
"rainfall" .PLU Rainfed crops, semi-arid to sub-humid
"thermal" .Tnx Temperate crops, frost- or heat-triggered

For sub-Sahelian contexts, onset = "rainfall" is the appropriate choice in virtually all situations.

Native AquaCrop rainfall criteria (1–4)

Criterion 1 — Cumulative rainfall since the start of the window

Onset is triggered as soon as cumulative rainfall since window_start reaches preset_value mm.

write_cal(
  site_name     = "Ouaga",
  onset         = "rainfall",
  window_start  = 100,
  window_length = 120,
  criterion     = 1,
  preset_value  = 25
)

A single heavy convective event can trigger onset even if the following two weeks are completely dry — a common pattern in the northern Sahel. Best suited to wetter agroclimatic zones where the onset, once triggered, tends to be sustained.

Criterion 2 — Observed rainfall over N consecutive days

Onset is triggered when total rainfall over successive_days consecutive days equals or exceeds preset_value mm.

write_cal(
  site_name       = "Ouaga",
  onset           = "rainfall",
  window_start    = 100,
  window_length   = 120,
  criterion       = 2,
  preset_value    = 20,
  successive_days = 3
)

This mirrors the agronomic rule used by extension services in Burkina Faso and Mali: ≥ 20 mm over 3 days provides sufficient soil moisture for germination of maize and sorghum. Setting occurrences = 2 requires the condition to be met twice before onset is declared — a simple but effective filter against isolated convective events.

Criterion 3 — 10-day (dekadal) rainfall

Onset is triggered when accumulated rainfall over the 10-day dekad containing day j equals or exceeds preset_value mm. Operationally aligned with the dekadal bulletins produced by national meteorological services across West Africa, including ANAM-BF.

write_cal(
  site_name     = "Ouaga",
  onset         = "rainfall",
  window_start  = 100,
  window_length = 120,
  criterion     = 3,
  preset_value  = 30
)

Criterion 4 — 10-day rainfall as a fraction of 10-day ETo

Requires an .ETo file. Onset is triggered when 10-day rainfall exceeds preset_value percent of the 10-day reference evapotranspiration. Accounts for atmospheric demand — conceptually superior to rainfall-only thresholds in semi-arid environments, but requiring careful calibration given that pre-monsoon ETo in the Sahel can reach 8–10 mm/day.

write_cal(
  site_name     = "Ouaga",
  onset         = "rainfall",
  window_start  = 100,
  window_length = 120,
  criterion     = 4,
  preset_value  = 50
)

Novel Sahelian criteria

All three methods below address a common gap in the native AquaCrop criteria: none of the built-in rules were designed with the specific rainfall dynamics of the Sahel in mind — namely, the frequency of false starts, the importance of post-germination dry spell risk, and the need for methods validated against farmer behaviour in the field.

Sivakumar (1988)

Sivakumar (1988) proposed one of the earliest operationally robust definitions of onset for West Africa: the first day after a threshold date when rainfall over a given number of days meets a minimum, provided no prolonged dry spell follows within a defined subsequent period. Built from long-term station data across the Sudanian and Sahelian zones, this definition directly captures the agronomic concern that a good start is one that holds.

Marteau et al. (2009)

Marteau et al. (2009) refined the Sivakumar definition using field observations of actual sowing decisions by pearl millet farmers in southwest Niger. Their formulation adjusts rainfall and dry spell thresholds based on agronomic field evidence rather than meteorological convention alone, anchoring the onset definition in observed farmer behaviour. It is particularly well suited for operational use in the Sahelian belt.

Criterion 5 — Fuzzy logic (Waongo et al., 2014)

Criteria 1 through 4, as well as the Sivakumar and Marteau approaches, share a structural limitation: they are binary. A cumulative rainfall of 24.9 mm does not trigger a criterion set at 25 mm, even though agronomically the two situations are nearly identical. This artificial cliff effect tends to exaggerate interannual variability in simulated planting dates and is particularly problematic in probabilistic forecasting contexts.

Criterion 5 addresses this by replacing hard thresholds with three fuzzy membership functions, each mapping a physical indicator to a planting suitability score in [0,1][0, 1]:

Component Symbol What it measures Direction
Cumulative rainfall over accum_days days γ1\gamma_1 Soil moisture recharge Increasing ↑
Number of wet days over accum_days days γ2\gamma_2 Temporal distribution of rain Increasing ↑
Longest dry spell in the 30 days after onset γ3\gamma_3 Post-germination risk Decreasing ↓

Each function is trapezoidal, defined by a lower and upper bound. Onset is declared on the first day jj for which:

γ1(j)×γ2(j)×γ3(j)≥threshold\gamma_1(j) \times \gamma_2(j) \times \gamma_3(j) \geq \text{threshold}

Because the index is a product, all three components must contribute simultaneously. Abundant rain concentrated in a single event (γ2\gamma_2 low), or rain immediately followed by a long dry spell (γ3\gamma_3 low), will not trigger onset even if the cumulative total is high. This makes criterion 5 the only method in aquacropr that explicitly and continuously penalises post-planting dry spell risk.

write_cal(
  site_name       = "Ouaga",
  onset           = "rainfall",
  window_start    = 100,
  window_length   = 120,
  criterion       = 5,
  preset_value    = 20,    # cum_rain_lower (mm)
  successive_days = 3,     # accum_days
  cum_rain_upper  = 40,
  wet_days_lower  = 1,
  wet_days_upper  = 3,
  dry_spell_lower = 7,
  dry_spell_upper = 15,
  fuzzy_threshold = 0.5
)

All eight parameters are written into the .CAL file and recovered automatically when find_onset() or write_prm() is called.

Thermal criteria

Native to AquaCrop, thermal criteria use the .Tnx file and are designed for temperature-limited growing seasons. They are not relevant for the Sahel but are fully available when aquacropr is used in temperate or highland contexts.

Criterion Trigger condition
1 Daily Tmin ≥ preset for N successive days
2 Daily Tmean ≥ preset for N successive days
3 Cumulative GDD ≥ preset over N successive days
4 Cumulative GDD since window start ≥ preset

Fixed onset

When the sowing date is prescribed — irrigated schemes, experimental designs, or climatological baselines — onset = "fixed" is the right choice.

write_cal(
  site_name = "Bama_irrigated",
  onset     = "fixed",
  fixed_day = 166   # June 15
)

find_onset() reads CLIMATE/<site_name>.PLU from the working directory. For multi-method comparisons on the same station, copy or symlink the same .PLU under each site_name.

Sahelian realities

False starts

The Sahelian rainy season is characterised by high intra- and inter-annual variability. False starts — isolated rainfall events followed by dry spells of 10 days or more — are frequent in May and early June, particularly north of the 12th parallel (Sultan et al., 2005). Criteria 1 and 3 do not penalise post-onset dry spells and are the most exposed to this risk. Criterion 2 with occurrences = 2, or any of the three Sahelian-specific methods, provide progressively stronger protection.

Indicative parameter ranges for criterion 5

Starting points derived from Waongo et al. (2014) and operational practice at ANAM-BF. Local calibration against observed planting dates is always recommended before operational use.

Parameter Sudan-Sahel (700–900 mm/yr) Sahel (400–700 mm/yr)
preset_value (cum_rain_lower) 15–20 mm 10–15 mm
cum_rain_upper 30–40 mm 25–35 mm
accum_days 3 days 3 days
wet_days_lower 1 1
wet_days_upper 3 2–3
dry_spell_lower 7–10 days 5–7 days
dry_spell_upper 15–20 days 10–15 days
fuzzy_threshold 0.4–0.6 0.3–0.5
window_start DOY 100 (Apr 10) DOY 120 (Apr 30)
window_length 120 days 100 days

Fallback behaviour

When no day in the search window satisfies the onset criterion, find_onset() returns the last day of the window (window_start + window_length - 1). This sentinel value ensures that simulations always receive a valid planting date. Years where the fallback is triggered should be flagged in your analyses as “no onset detected” and not interpreted as genuine late onsets.

References

Marteau, R., Sultan, B., Moron, V., Guilderson, T., Kane, C., & Traoré, S. B. (2009). The onset of the rainy season and farmers’ sowing strategy for pearl millet cultivation in Southwest Niger. Agricultural and Forest Meteorology, 148(10), 1738–1750.

Sivakumar, M. V. K. (1988). Predicting rainy season potential from the onset of rains in Southern Sahelian and Sudanian climatic zones of West Africa. Agricultural and Forest Meteorology, 42(4), 295–305.

Sultan, B., Baron, C., Dingkuhn, M., Sarr, B., & Janicot, S. (2005). Agricultural impacts of large-scale variability of the West African monsoon. Agricultural and Forest Meteorology, 128(1–2), 93–110.

Waongo, M., Laux, P., Traoré, S. B., Sanon, M., & Kunstmann, H. (2014). A crop model and fuzzy rule based approach for optimizing maize planting dates in Burkina Faso, West Africa. Journal of Applied Meteorology and Climatology, 53(3), 598–613.
